data.title: Lenovo hits highest PC market share during Q3; CEO says memory crunch will persist
data.description:
Lenovo's (LNVGY)(LNVGF) third-quarter revenue climbed 18% year over year to reach a new, all-time high as the company reported significant growth in artificial intelligence-related demand and growing market share in PC and smart devices.

Revenue for the quarter ended December 31 totaled $22.2B, which was much more than the consensus estimate of $20.6B.

Lenovo reported that its PC market share reached 25.2% in the third quarter, which is the only time any company has surpassed 25% global PC market share. 

"We expanded our global PC market share to 25.3%, up 1 percentage point year-on-year, extending our lead over our closest competitor by 5 percentage points and marking our second consecutive quarter as the only vendor to surpass 25% global PC market share since IDC data became available," said Lenovo Chief Financial Officer Shao-Min Cheng.

The company's primary competitors in the PC market include Dell (DELL), HP (HPQ) and Apple (AAPL).

Lenovo's infrastructure solutions group segment increased revenue by 31% year over year to $5.2B. The growth was fueled by its AI server business, which had a $15.5B pipeline exiting the quarter. Lenovo also recently launched AI inferencing servers and the Lenovo AI Cloud Gigafactory with Nvidia (NVDA).

"Just as I shared last quarter, the infrastructure market is undergoing an important shift from AI training on public cloud to AI inferencing, increasingly happening on-prem and at the edge for enterprises," said Lenovo CEO Yang Yuanqing during the earnings call. "To better capture this trend, we carried out a strategic restructuring. This initiative is designed to boost the productivity by optimizing our cost structure, address market opportunities by refining our product portfolio, and enhance competitiveness by optimizing our business models and upgrading our sales force."

Yuanqing also discussed the ongoing memory supply shortage and staggering price increases.

"The DRAM cost increased by 50% last quarter, but the current quarter versus last quarter almost doubled again even with the contract price," he said. "So this structural imbalance between supply and demand is not simply a short-term fluctuation. It's likely to have a prolonged impact on the industry throughout this year. So we are closely monitoring the situation and taking agile action as necessary."

Lenovo's competitors, HP and Dell, were down 5% and 8%, respectively.
